executiveSummary:
**Executive Summary: Medicaid Program Performance Analysis**

**Enrollment Trends and Financial Overview**

National Medicaid enrollment stands at 75.3 million beneficiaries as of January 2026, with the program demonstrating widespread contraction following the conclusion of pandemic-era continuous enrollment provisions. Year-over-year enrollment declines are evident across major states, ranging from moderate decreases of 1.8% in New York to more substantial reductions of 7.6% in Michigan. Texas, with 4.0 million enrollees, experienced a 3.1% decline, mirroring the national normalization trend. Total program expenditures reached $215.9 billion in fiscal year 2024, yielding a national per-enrollee cost of $2,869. Notably, per-enrollee spending varies dramatically across states, with Pennsylvania ($4,377) and Massachusetts ($4,201) representing high-cost outliers, while Florida maintains the lowest per-enrollee expenditure at $2,031. Texas falls near the national average at $2,988 per enrollee, despite operating the fourth-largest state program by total spending at $12.0 billion.

**Quality Performance and State Comparisons**

Quality measure performance reveals significant interstate variation, with a national average score of 65.6 out of 100 points in 2024. Ten states achieved high-performer status (≥75 points), led by Massachusetts (81) and Minnesota (80), while 15 states scored below 60 points, creating a concerning quality gap. Texas ranks 30th nationally with a quality score of 62, placing it in the moderate performance tier but below the national average. This positioning suggests opportunities for targeted quality improvement initiatives, particularly when compared to peer large states. The state's managed care penetration rate of 93.3% significantly exceeds the national average of 83.2%, indicating strong care coordination infrastructure that could be leveraged for quality enhancement. Recent federal regulatory activity, including proposed 2028 HCBS quality measures and ongoing OIG oversight activities, signals continued federal emphasis on accountability and performance measurement that will require state-level strategic response and resource allocation adjustments.
texasSpotlight:
## Texas Medicaid Program Performance Analysis

Texas operates the nation's third-largest Medicaid program with 4.03 million enrollees, representing approximately 5.4% of national enrollment despite having 9% of the U.S. population—a coverage gap reflecting the state's non-expansion status. The program's -3.1% year-over-year enrollment decline aligns closely with national post-pandemic unwinding trends and matches Pennsylvania's decline rate, while performing better than larger peers like California (-6.2%) and Illinois (-6.2%). Texas demonstrates operational efficiency with per-enrollee expenditures of $2,988, falling between the national average of $2,869 and remaining significantly below expansion states like New York ($3,431) and Pennsylvania ($4,377). The state's aggressive managed care strategy shows strong results, with 93.3% penetration率 substantially exceeding the national average of 83.2%, suggesting effective care coordination and cost management mechanisms.

However, quality outcomes present ongoing challenges, with Texas scoring 62/100 on CMS quality measures—ranking 30th nationally and falling below the national average of 65.6. While avoiding the bottom tier occupied by neighboring southern states like Arkansas (54) and Oklahoma (51), Texas trails North Carolina (which likely benefits from Medicaid expansion) and other large state peers in care quality metrics. The state's position among non-expansion states creates inherent coverage limitations, particularly for adults aged 19-64, which may contribute to fragmented care patterns and suboptimal population health outcomes. Key areas to monitor include whether Texas's high managed care penetration can drive quality improvements over time, how the state addresses coverage gaps without expansion, and potential federal regulatory changes around HCBS quality measures and data collection requirements that could impact program operations and reporting.

alerts.title: Pennsylvania's Per-Enrollee Spending 53% Above National Average
alerts.description: Pennsylvania's per-enrollee spending of $4,377 is significantly higher than the national average of $2,869, indicating potential cost control issues.

alerts.title: Florida's Exceptionally Low Per-Enrollee Spending Raises Questions
alerts.description: Florida's per-enrollee spending of $2,031 is 29% below the national average despite having 3.6M enrollees, potentially indicating access or adequacy concerns.

alerts.title: Five States Show Critically Low Quality Performance
alerts.description: Mississippi, Oklahoma, Alabama, South Carolina, and Arkansas scored below 55 on quality measures, with Mississippi at only 48 points.

alerts.title: Massachusetts: High Spending with High Quality Performance
alerts.description: Massachusetts combines the highest per-enrollee spending ($4,201) with top-tier quality performance (81/100), demonstrating effective resource utilization.

alerts.title: 15 States Performing Below Quality Expectations
alerts.description: Nearly 30% of states (15 out of 50) scored below 60 on quality measures, indicating widespread performance concerns across the program.

executiveInsights.title: Texas Quality Score at Risk - Below National Average at 62/100
executiveInsights.summary: Texas ranks #30 of 50 states with a quality score of 62/100, falling below the national average of 65.6. This places Texas in the moderate tier, significantly behind top performers like MA (81) and MN (80).
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Quality performance directly impacts contract renewals and federal oversight. Poor quality scores can trigger CMS intervention and affect Accenture's reputation on this high-profile contract.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag quality improvement initiatives to HHSC leadership immediately

executiveInsights.title: Texas Medicaid Enrollment Declining 3.1% YoY to 4.03M
executiveInsights.summary: Texas experienced a 3.1% year-over-year enrollment decline, dropping to 4,032,864 enrollees. This matches the national trend but represents significant membership loss in our largest contract state.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Enrollment declines directly impact revenue projections and may signal redetermination issues or eligibility processing problems that Accenture systems need to address.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review enrollment processing systems and redetermination workflows with HHSC

executiveInsights.title: Texas Per-Enrollee Costs Above National Average at $2,988
executiveInsights.summary: Texas spends $2,988 per enrollee compared to the national average of $2,869, with total expenditures of $12.05B. This is higher than comparable large states like Illinois ($2,715) and North Carolina ($2,616).
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Higher per-enrollee costs may indicate inefficiencies in care management or utilization that Accenture's managed care systems should be optimizing to demonstrate contract value.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review cost management strategies and utilization patterns with managed care plans

executiveInsights.title: Texas Managed Care Penetration Exceeds National Average at 93.3%
executiveInsights.summary: Texas achieves 93.3% managed care penetration with 3.86M enrollees, significantly above the national average of 83.2%. This represents strong managed care adoption in our contract state.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: High managed care penetration validates Accenture's systems architecture and provides leverage for demonstrating success to other state prospects.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Leverage Texas MC success metrics for other state business development

executiveInsights.title: New HCBS Quality Measures Incoming for 2028 Implementation
executiveInsights.summary: CMS published new Medicaid Home and Community-Based Services Quality Measure Set for 2028 implementation. This represents upcoming regulatory changes affecting quality reporting requirements.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: New quality measures will require system updates and reporting modifications that Accenture needs to plan for to maintain compliance and avoid contract penalties.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review 2028 HCBS quality measure requirements and assess system impact

riskOpportunity.label: Enrollment decline management
riskOpportunity.description: Texas Medicaid enrollment dropped 3.1% YoY, creating budget pressures and potential system optimization needs.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop enrollment stabilization and cost optimization strategies for HHSC.

riskOpportunity.label: Quality measure improvement
riskOpportunity.description: Texas ranks #30 nationally with 62/100 quality score, presenting significant improvement potential.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Propose comprehensive quality improvement program to move Texas into top performer tier.

riskOpportunity.label: Managed care recompete
riskOpportunity.description: High 93.3% managed care penetration in Texas creates major procurement cycle exposure and opportunity.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Monitor HHSC procurement portal and prepare for managed care contract recompetition.

riskOpportunity.label: HCBS quality measures
riskOpportunity.description: New 2028 HCBS quality measure requirements will require significant system and process changes.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Position Accenture as HCBS quality measurement implementation partner for Texas.

riskOpportunity.label: OIG oversight exposure
riskOpportunity.description: Active HHS OIG Medicaid oversight creates compliance risk but also positions Accenture as trusted advisor.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Strengthen compliance monitoring and audit readiness capabilities for HHSC.

riskOpportunity.label: Per-enrollee cost efficiency
riskOpportunity.description: Texas $2,988 per-enrollee cost is above national average, creating pressure for cost management solutions.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop comprehensive cost reduction and efficiency improvement program for Texas Medicaid.

riskOpportunity.label: Technology modernization wave
riskOpportunity.description: Multiple CMS regulatory changes and data collection requirements drive need for system upgrades.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Package integrated technology modernization proposal addressing multiple regulatory requirements.

riskOpportunity.label: Competitive displacement risk
riskOpportunity.description: Multiple procurement portals show active bidding environment with potential for contract losses.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Implement systematic competitive intelligence and bid response capability enhancement.
